text: - I'm Brian Cox,
I'm a professor of particle physics
at the University of Manchester
and the Royal Society professor
for public engagement in science.
And amongst other things,
I'm the author of several science books,
including "Black Holes:
The Key to Understanding the Universe".
- [Narrator] Chapter one,
Black Holes and the Edge of Physics.
- Could black holes be the key
to a quantum theory of gravity,
a deeper theory of how reality,
of how space and time works?
Well, I think so.
It's interesting.
These objects, which have been known,
I would say, for 40 or 50 years,

but theoretically for the
best part of a century,
have always been fascinating.
They're odd things.
The simplest way to describe a black hole
would be a region of space
from which even light can't escape.
Predictions that such objects existed

go all the way back to the
beginnings of relativity
back at the turn of the 20th century.

But actually really I
would say into the 1960s,

perhaps even into the
1980s, many physicists felt

that because of the
intellectual challenges
that these predicted objects pose,

Black holes are interesting,

work of Stephen Hawking

French mathematician Laplace.

in terms of an idea

a planet or a star.
For the Earth, for example,

the surface of the Earth

11 kilometers a second.
